questions.text: What is the key difference between let and const in JavaScript?
opts: let is block-scoped; const is function-scoped.
let can be reassigned; const cannot be reassigned after declaration.
const variables are always faster to access.
There is no difference — they are interchangeable.
questions.exp:
var — the old way. Function-scoped, can be re-declared, has hoisting behaviour that causes subtle bugs. Avoid it in modern JavaScript.

let — block-scoped, can be reassigned but not re-declared in the same scope.

const — block-scoped, the binding cannot be reassigned after declaration. Important: if a const holds an object or array, the contents can still be changed — only the variable reference is locked.

Rule of thumb: default to const, use let when you know the value will change, never use var.

questions.text: JavaScript is described as 'dynamically typed.' Which statement best explains what this means?
opts: JavaScript runs faster than statically typed languages.
Variable types are declared upfront and checked by a compiler before the code runs.
A variable has no declared type — its type is determined at runtime by the value it holds.
JavaScript automatically converts all values to strings.
questions.exp:
In a statically typed language like C#, you declare the type when you create a variable: int age = 25. The compiler checks types at build time and rejects mismatches before the code ever runs.

In a dynamically typed language like JavaScript, you write let age = 25 — no declared type. The same variable can later be reassigned to a string or boolean with no error. The type is determined at runtime based on whatever value the variable holds at that moment.

The trade-off: C# catches type errors early and IDE tooling works better. JavaScript is more flexible but type bugs only surface when the code actually runs. TypeScript was built specifically to bring static typing to JavaScript.

questions.text: What does "5" + 3 evaluate to in JavaScript, and why?
opts: 8 — JavaScript converts the string to a number.
"53" — JavaScript converts the number to a string and concatenates.
NaN — mixing types always causes an error.
undefined — the operation is invalid.
questions.exp:
The result is "53" — a string. This is called type coercion: JavaScript automatically converts types to make an operation work rather than throwing an error.

When + sees a string on either side, it treats the whole operation as string concatenation and converts the other operand to a string. So "5" + 3 becomes "5" + "3" = "53".

The reverse is instructive: "5" - 3 gives 2, because - has no string meaning, so JavaScript converts "5" to a number instead.

+ is the one to watch — it doubles as both addition and concatenation, making it the most common source of coercion bugs.

questions.text: What is the difference between null and undefined in JavaScript?
opts: They are identical — both mean a variable has no value.
null means a variable was never declared; undefined means it was declared but empty.
undefined means a variable was declared but never assigned; null is an intentional empty value set by the developer.
null is a number type; undefined is a string type.
questions.exp:
Both represent the absence of a value, but they come from different places.

undefined — JavaScript sets this automatically when a variable is declared but never assigned a value. It means 'I don't know what this is yet.'

null — a developer explicitly assigns this to signal 'this is deliberately empty.' It means 'I know this is nothing.'

A practical example: if you look up a user in a database and they don't exist, you might intentionally return null. A variable you forgot to assign is undefined unintentionally.

One famous quirk: typeof null returns "object" — this is a long-standing JavaScript bug that was never fixed for backwards compatibility reasons.

questions.text: The __________ operator returns a string describing the type of a value at runtime — for example, it returns "number" for 42 and "string" for "hello".
questions.hint: A single keyword operator — not a function call
questions.exp:
typeof is an operator that returns a string describing the type of a value. Common results:

typeof 42 → "number"
typeof "hello" → "string"
typeof true → "boolean"
typeof undefined → "undefined"
typeof {} → "object"
typeof [] → "object" (arrays are objects in JS)
typeof function(){} → "function"
typeof null → "object" (the famous bug)

You'd use it when you need to check what type something is before operating on it — for example, confirming a function parameter is actually a number before doing arithmetic with it.

questions.text: A function calculates a result but the caller always receives undefined. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The function has too many parameters.
The function is missing a return statement.
The variable name is spelled incorrectly.
JavaScript functions cannot produce values.
questions.exp:
return does two things: it immediately exits the function, and it sends a value back to the caller.

Without a return statement — or with a bare return — the function still runs to completion but sends back undefined. The calculation happens and then disappears.

Example of the mistake:
function add(a, b) { a + b; } — result is lost, caller gets undefined

Correct:
function add(a, b) { return a + b; }

Once return executes, no further code in that function runs — it exits immediately. A function can only return one value, but that value can be an object or array containing multiple things.

questions.text: You pass an object to a function and the function modifies one of its properties. Back outside the function, the original object has changed. Why?
opts: JavaScript automatically makes a deep copy of all objects passed to functions.
Objects are passed by reference — the function receives a pointer to the same object in memory.
This only happens with const objects.
Functions always return the modified version of their arguments.
questions.exp:
JavaScript passes values to functions in two different ways depending on the type.

Primitives (numbers, strings, booleans) are passed by value — the function receives a copy. Changing it inside the function has no effect on the original variable outside.

Objects and arrays are passed by reference — the function receives a pointer to the same location in memory. If the function modifies a property, the original object is changed too.

Example:
function addYear(user) { user.age++; }
const person = { age: 25 };
addYear(person);
console.log(person.age); // 26 — the original changed!

This surprises many beginners. To avoid mutating the original, you'd create a copy first: const copy = { ...user };

questions.text: What is the difference between a class and an object?
opts: A class holds real data; an object is an empty template.
They are the same thing — 'class' and 'object' are interchangeable terms.
A class is a blueprint or template; an object is a specific instance created from that blueprint.
A class can only be used once; an object can be reused many times.
questions.exp:
A class is a blueprint — it defines what properties and methods something will have, but holds no actual data itself.

An object is a specific instance created from that blueprint — it holds real values.

Real-world analogy: a class is like the architectural plans for a house. An object is an actual house built from those plans. You can build many houses (objects) from the same plans (class).

In JavaScript:
class Car {
  constructor(make) { this.make = make; }
}

const myCar = new Car("Toyota"); — this creates an object, a specific car with real data.

A C# developer will find this familiar: classes in C# work the same way conceptually, though JavaScript's underlying mechanism (prototypal inheritance) differs from C#'s classical inheritance.

questions.text: In a for loop written as for (let i = 0; i < 4; i++), how many times does the loop body run?
opts: 3 times
4 times
5 times
Infinitely
questions.exp:
The loop body runs 4 times, with i taking the values 0, 1, 2, and 3.

A for loop has three parts in the parentheses:

Initialiser — runs once before the loop starts: let i = 0
Condition — checked before every iteration; loop runs while true, stops when false: i < 4
Increment — runs after each iteration: i++

When i reaches 4, the condition i < 4 is false, so the loop exits. The body never runs with i = 4.

Use a for loop when you know in advance how many times something should run. For iterating over an array's elements, for...of is often cleaner. For iterating over an object's keys, use for...in.

questions.text: When is a switch statement generally preferred over if/else?
opts: When the condition involves a range or complex boolean expression.
When you are checking one variable against multiple specific fixed values.
When you need to run a block of code only once.
switch is always preferred — it runs faster.
questions.exp:
Both branch code based on conditions, but they serve different use cases.

if/else — flexible, each condition can be any boolean expression including ranges and complex logic. Use it for: if (age > 18 && hasId), if (score >= 90), or any condition that isn't a simple exact match.

switch — designed for checking one variable against multiple specific fixed values. It's cleaner and more readable when you have many exact cases. Use it for: mapping a status code to a message, handling different user roles, routing based on a command string.

Important: switch requires a break statement at the end of each case. Without it, execution 'falls through' into the next case — a common bug.

switch (status) {
  case 404: message = 'Not found'; break;
  case 500: message = 'Server error'; break;
}

questions.text: What does break do when used inside a for loop?
opts: It skips the current iteration and moves to the next one.
It pauses the loop for one second.
It immediately exits the loop entirely, regardless of remaining iterations.
It resets the loop counter back to zero.
questions.exp:
break immediately exits the loop entirely. No remaining iterations run, and execution jumps to the code after the loop.

It's useful when you've found what you were looking for and continuing would be wasteful:
for (let i = 0; i < users.length; i++) {
  if (users[i].id === targetId) {
    found = users[i];
    break; // no point checking the rest
  }
}

The related keyword is continue — it skips only the current iteration and moves to the next one, without exiting the loop entirely.

In a switch statement, break serves a different but related role — preventing execution from falling through into the next case.

questions.text: What is an API, and how do you consume one from JavaScript in a browser?
opts: An API is a database table — you query it with SQL.
An API is a defined contract for two systems to communicate; in JavaScript you consume it using fetch() to send HTTP requests and receive JSON responses.
An API is a JavaScript framework like React or Vue.
An API is only accessible from server-side languages like C# or Python, not from a browser.
questions.exp:
An API (Application Programming Interface) is a defined contract that lets two systems communicate. A web API exposes endpoints — URLs that accept requests and return data, almost always as JSON.

In JavaScript you consume one using fetch:
fetch('https://api.example.com/users')
  .then(r => r.json())
  .then(data => console.log(data));

The browser sends an HTTP request → the server processes it → sends back JSON → your JavaScript reads the data and does something with it.

In a cloud support role, APIs are everywhere. Customers integrate their applications with cloud services via APIs, and many support issues come down to a misconfigured API call — wrong endpoint, missing auth header, malformed JSON body, or incorrect HTTP method.

questions.text: To convert a JSON string received from an API into a usable JavaScript object, you use JSON.__________( ).
questions.exp:
JSON.parse(str) converts a JSON string into a usable JavaScript object.
JSON.stringify(obj) converts a JavaScript object into a JSON string — used when sending data to a server.

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text format for transporting structured data. It looks like a JavaScript object but is a plain string. Rules: all keys must be in double quotes, values can be strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, objects, or null — but not functions or undefined.

Example:
const str = '{"name": "Alice", "age": 30}';
const user = JSON.parse(str);
console.log(user.name); // "Alice"

JSON is the universal language of web APIs — almost every API you'll work with sends and receives JSON.

questions.text: In a web application, what is the difference between a client and a server?
opts: The client stores the database; the server displays the user interface.
They are the same machine — 'client' and 'server' are just different names for the same thing.
The client is the application making the request (e.g. a browser); the server receives the request, processes it, and sends back a response.
The client is always faster than the server.
questions.exp:
The client is the device or application making a request — typically a browser, a mobile app, or a script.

The server is the machine that receives the request, processes it, and sends back a response.

In web development: the browser (client) requests a webpage → the web server sends back HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

In an API context: your JavaScript code (client) calls an endpoint → the backend (server) handles the logic and returns data.

This distinction is critical in cloud support. When something breaks, the first question is always: is the problem on the client side (bad request, missing auth header, wrong data sent, CORS issue) or the server side (crash, outage, database error, misconfiguration)? The HTTP status code — 4xx vs 5xx — immediately tells you which side to investigate.

questions.text: What does HTTP stand for, and what is the key difference between a GET and a POST request?
opts: HyperText Transfer Protocol — GET sends data to the server; POST retrieves data.
HyperText Transfer Protocol — GET retrieves data without changing server state; POST sends data in the request body to create or submit something.
High Transfer Text Protocol — GET and POST are identical but GET is faster.
HyperText Transfer Protocol — GET is for images; POST is for text.
questions.exp:
HTTP stands for HyperText Transfer Protocol — the rules governing how data is sent between client and server on the web.

GET — requests data from the server. Parameters go in the URL. No request body. Should not change anything on the server (read-only). Example: loading a webpage, fetching a list of users.

POST — sends data to the server. Payload goes in the request body. Used when creating or submitting something. Example: submitting a form, creating a new record, logging in.

Analogy: GET is asking a question; POST is handing something in.

Other methods worth knowing: PUT updates an existing resource entirely, PATCH partially updates one, DELETE removes one. Together these form the basis of REST API design.

questions.text: A user reports a 404 error. What does this tell you, and how is it different from a 500 error?
opts: Both mean the server crashed — they are interchangeable.
404 means the request was too large; 500 means the password was wrong.
404 is a client-side problem — the requested resource doesn't exist at that URL. 500 is a server-side problem — the server failed to process a valid request.
404 means no internet connection; 500 means the database is full.
questions.exp:
HTTP status codes are grouped by their first digit, which immediately tells you which side has the problem.

4xx — client-side errors (the request itself was wrong):
400 Bad Request — server couldn't understand the request, usually malformed data
401 Unauthorized — not authenticated
403 Forbidden — authenticated but no permission
404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist at that URL
429 Too Many Requests — rate limit exceeded

5xx — server-side errors (the request was fine but the server failed):
500 Internal Server Error — generic server crash
502 Bad Gateway — upstream server issue
503 Service Unavailable — server is down or overloaded

In support: 4xx means investigate the client's request. 5xx means investigate the server or cloud infrastructure.

questions.text: A user contacts support and says: 'The website is down.' Walk through the questions you would ask first and explain why each one matters.
questions.answer: Isolate the scope (everyone or just them?), identify the symptom (error code, blank page, timeout?), check for recent changes, attempt to reproduce, then escalate with evidence.
questions.exp:
The goal is to isolate the problem systematically rather than guessing. Each question eliminates a category of cause.

'Is it down for everyone or just you?' — rules out a full outage vs a local issue (ISP, VPN, DNS, device-specific).

'What exactly are you seeing?' — a specific error code (404, 500, 503), a blank page, a timeout, or a partial load all point to different problems.

'What browser and device?' — rules out a browser-specific bug or a cached broken state (Ctrl+Shift+R hard refresh).

'Has anything changed recently?' — new deployment, config change, DNS update, or API key rotation are common causes.

'Can you reproduce it yourself?' — reproducing it is the most important step. If you can reproduce it, you can diagnose it.

In a cloud context add: is it affecting a specific region? A specific service endpoint? Does the cloud provider have an active incident on their status page?

Never assume — gather evidence first, then diagnose. Escalate with a clear description of what you found.

questions.text: Why should API keys and database connection strings be stored as environment variables rather than written directly in the code?
opts: Environment variables load faster than values written in code.
To keep secrets out of source code (security) and allow the same codebase to behave differently across environments without changing code.
Because JavaScript cannot read values that are written directly in a file.
Environment variables are required by all cloud platforms — there is no alternative.
questions.exp:
Environment variables are configuration values stored in the server or cloud environment rather than hardcoded in the application's source code.

Two core reasons to use them:

1. Security — if an API key or database password is written in the code and committed to a repository (even a private one), it can be leaked. Environment variables keep secrets out of source control entirely.

2. Flexibility — the same codebase can point to a test database in development and a production database in production, simply by changing the environment variable — no code change needed.

In Node.js: process.env.API_KEY

In cloud platforms like AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud, environment variables are configured per deployment and injected into the running application. A common support issue is a customer who has rotated an API key but forgotten to update the environment variable in their cloud deployment.
